rulings.Case Title: CITY OF MOUNTAIN VIEW CALIFORNIA A PUBLIC ENTITY et al VS. DAORO ZYDEL & HOLLAND A PUBLIC ACCOUNTANCY FIRM et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON TUESDAY, AUGUST 28, 2007, LINE 7. DEFENDANTS DAORO ZYDEL & HOLLAND, ROBERT YOSHIOKA, WILSON, MCCALL & DAORO'S FIRM MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT: Defendants' motion for summary judgment based on the running of the statute of limitations is granted. Defendants' undisputed facts, C 1-39, establish that the City was aware of facts that gave rise to its reasonable suspicion that SAP was not making the payments required by the lease at least as early as 2001 if not before. Since the defendant accounting firm was responsible for preparing the Annual Reconciliation Reports to determine whether the requisite payments had been made, the City was on notice that it was receiving less revenue than it should and that the accountants were not properly discharging their oversight responsibilities. The decision in Apple Valley Unified School District v. Vavrinek, Trine, Day & Co. (2002) 98 Cal.App.4th 934 involved similar facts, in particular that the accounting firm had been hired by plaintiff, and the statute of limitations was held to bar the claim. Plaintiffs' contention that it could not discover the wrongdoing is negated by the decision in Jolly v. Eli Lilly & Co. (1988) 44 Cal.3d 1103, 11110-1112, involving a far more compelling set of facts for plaintiff. In this case, it appears that the material facts that arose from the Yoshioka deposition related to a lack of independence on the part of the accountants. Although important, that is not a basis for tolling the statute of limitations because the City was on notice of a potential claim long before the deposition. Snapp & Associates Ins. Services,Inc. v. Robertson (2002) 96 Cal.App.4th 884, 890-891. Plaintiffs' motion to strike Yoshioka Declaration 2:18-21 is granted in part, striking the second sentence. =(302/PJM)
